title: Causal sequence-to-hazard model over each driver's race history
summary: Recent survival papers show recurrent/transformer hazard models handle longitudinal, non-proportional event risk better than static tabular models.
rationale: Recent survival papers show recurrent/transformer hazard models handle longitudinal, non-proportional event risk better than static tabular models. Prior work already explored hazard reformulation; the new angle is a strictly causal sequence expert that consumes the whole within-race lap trajectory and outputs next-lap stop hazard.
implementation_hint: Train a compact GRU/Transformer on ordered laps within each (Year,Race,Driver), using masking and past-only features; predict next-lap hazard directly or a short horizon PMF, then stack its out-of-fold predictions with the boosted-tree baseline. Keep folds grouped by race to prevent the model from memorizing session-specific dynamics.
expected_effect: Can capture temporal motifs like tyre reset, repeated pace fade, and post-pit cooldown that hand-crafted ratios miss.
risk: Sequence models on 439k rows can overfit or underperform strong GBDTs if regularization and grouped CV are weak.
